coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of long-wavelength emission carbon dots for hypochlorite sensing and intracellular pH imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Meng, Yating
Wu, Linzhu
Zhao, Junxiu
Shuang, Shaomin
Dong, Chuan
Nie, Jisheng
coredata.dc:description: Hypochlorite (ClO-), a typical reactive oxygen species, plays an irreplaceable roles in various biological processes. In this work, long-wavelength emission carbon dots (LW-CDs) were fabricated through one-step hydrothermal method by using l-cysteine (cys) and neutral red (NR) as precursors for monitoring of hypochlorite and intracellular pH. Characterizations of as-prepared LW-CDs showed that they had excellent water solubility, high optical stability and sensitive response behavior. Fluorescence intensity of LW-CDs decayed in the presence of ClO- linearly from 10 to 162.5 μM (LOD = 1.021 μM) based on static quenching effect with ideal selectivity. Besides, LW-CDs revealed a pH responsive behavior in the pH range of 2.0 to 10.0, exhibited dual good linear relationships in the pH ranges of 4.2–5.8 and 5.8–7.4. The LW-CDs can also be utilized as imaging reagents in Hela living cells owing excellent biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity. These results demonstrated that the as-mentioned LW-CDs are expected to serve as excellent long wavelength emitting nanomaterials for fluorescence sensing and monitoring of cell fluctuations.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Long-wavelength emission
Carbon dots
pH variation
Hypochlorite sensing
Cell imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a)TEM and HRTEM(inset) image of LW-CDs; (b) the size distribution of LW-CDs; (c) AFM image of LW-CDs.
(a) FTIR spectrum; (b) Full XPS spectrum; (c) C 1 s XPS spectrum; (d) N 1 s XPS spectrum; (e) O 1 s XPS spectrum; (f) S 2p XPS spectrum of the LW-CDs.
(a) the UV/Vis, excitation and emission spectra of the LW-CDs; (b) the emission spectra of the LW-CDs when excited by different wavelength.

(a) The fluorescence spectrum of the LW-CDs when pH varies from 2.0 to 10.0; (b) The relationship of PL intensity and pH; (c)The linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and pH in the range of 4.2–5.8; (d) The linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and pH in the range of 5.8––7.4.

(a) The fluorescence spectrum of the LW-CDs when concentration of ClO- varies from 0 to 300 μM; (b) The relationship of (F0-F)/F0 and concentration of ClO-; (c) The linear equation of (F0-F)/F0 and concentration of ClO-; (d) The influence of different ions on the fluorescence intensity of the LW-CDs; (e) Fluorescence lifetime curve of LW-CDs and LW-CDs with ClO-; (f) UV/Vis spectra of LW-CDs and LW-CDs with ClO-.
The LSCM images of LW-CDs in Hela cells at pH 4, 7 and 9.
The LSCM images of LW-CDs in Hela cells treated with LW-CDs and LW-CDs/ClO-.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of long-wavelength emission carbon dots for hypochlorite sensing and intracellular pH imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Occupational Health, School of Public Health, Shanxi Key Laboratory of Environmental Health Impairment and Prevention, NHC Key Laboratory of Pneumoconiosis, MOE Key Laboratory of Coal Environmental Pathogenicity and Prevention, Shanxi Medical University, Taiyuan, 030001 China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanxi Key Laboratory of Environmental Health Impairment and Prevention
MOE Key Laboratory of Coal Environmental Pathogenicity and Prevention

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Occupational Health, School of Public Health, Shanxi Key Laboratory of Environmental Health Impairment and Prevention, NHC Key Laboratory of Pneumoconiosis, MOE Key Laboratory of Coal Environmental Pathogenicity and Prevention, Shanxi Medical University, Taiyuan, 030001 China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hypochlorite (ClO-), a typical reactive oxygen species, plays an irreplaceable roles in various biological processes. In this work, long-wavelength emission carbon dots (LW-CDs) were fabricated through one-step hydrothermal method by using l-cysteine (cys) and neutral red (NR) as precursors for monitoring of hypochlorite and intracellular pH. Characterizations of as-prepared LW-CDs showed that they had excellent water solubility, high optical stability and sensitive response behavior. Fluorescence intensity of LW-CDs decayed in the presence of ClO- linearly from 10 to 162.5 μM (LOD = 1.021 μM) based on static quenching effect with ideal selectivity. Besides, LW-CDs revealed a pH responsive behavior in the pH range of 2.0 to 10.0, exhibited dual good linear relationships in the pH ranges of 4.2–5.8 and 5.8–7.4. The LW-CDs can also be utilized as imaging reagents in Hela living cells owing excellent biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity. These results demonstrated that the as-mentioned LW-CDs are expected to serve as excellent long wavelength emitting nanomaterials for fluorescence sensing and monitoring of cell fluctuations.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence analysis, as an advanced analytical method, has the advantages of high sensitivity, strong selectivity, and easy use, which has a wider range of applications and better popularity than methods such as electron probe, mass spectrometry, spectroscopy, and polarography [1–3]. The common fluorescence analysis methods mainly include fluorescence spectrophotometry [4], atomic fluorescence analysis [5], chemiluminescence analysis [6], time-resolved fluorescence analysis [7], and fluorescence polarization analysis [8]. Fluorescence spectrophotometry is a method of substance identification and content determination based on the position and intensity of the fluorescence spectrum lines of a substance [9]. Due to the different compositions and structures of different substances, the wavelengths of absorbed UV visible light and emitted light are also different. By comparing the shape and position of the excitation and fluorescence spectra of unknown substances with the spectra of standard substances, qualitative analysis can be performed. If the concentration of the substance is different, the fluorescence intensity it emits will be different, and measuring the fluorescence intensity of the substance can quantitatively determine it [10]. Compared to other fluorescence analysis methods, fluorescence spectrophotometry has significant advantages in reducing sample size and being easy to operate [11]. At present, a series of fluorescent probes based on fluorescence spectrophotometry have been developed for the detection of metal ions [12], anions [13], amino acids [14], and small biomolecules [15], which mainly including organic probes, inorganic and nano probes. Organic fluorescent probes include fluorescein probes [16,17], rhodamine probes [18,19], cyanine dyes probes [20,21], BODIPY probes [22,23], and coumarin fluorescent probes [24,25]. Inorganic and nanoprobes are mainly composed of CdS quantum dots [26], MoS2 quantum dots [27], CdTe quantum dots [28], ZnO quantum dots [29], graphene quantum dots [30], carbon quantum dots [31], and other fluorescent quantum dots. A variety of fluorescent probes have been used to construct analysis methods based on fluorescence intensity.

Carbon Quantum Dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional nanomaterials, which attracts massive attention because of their high biocompatibility, good optical properties, easy modification and high stability [32]. Generally, the size of CDs is less than 10 nm and the surface of CDs have a variety of functional groups, which result in some unique characters of CDs, such as quantum confinement and edge effects [33]. Up to date, CDs have been widely used in fluorescence detection, biological imaging, and biomedicine [34–36]. The functional groups on the surface of CDs enable to interact with some analytes by specific mechanism, leading to change of fluorescence signal of CDs, so CDs are promising candidates for fluorescence detection of many substances [37], such as ions [38–42] and organic small molecules [43–46]. However, most of CDs emit blue or green light, indicating that the penetration capacity into organism of these CDs is not promising. On the other hand, those CDs are heavily subjected to auto-fluorescence effect from tissues when used into biology imaging, limiting their further application [47,48]. Long-wavelength emission (orange to red) CDs could excellently avoid auto-fluorescence interference, and have better penetration ability into organism [49]. Therefore, the development of carbon dots with long-wavelength emission as detection platform is of important concern.

Hypochlorite is one of common reactive oxygen species (ROS) related to many physiological processes [50]. In biological system, optimal concentration of hypochlorite could help to promote the fusion of intracellular lysosomes and phagocytes. Besides, when biological system confronts invasion of microorganisms, endogenous hypochlorite is released through myeloperoxidase enzyme from activated neutrophils to prevent pathogen [51–54]. Generally, the endogenous hypochlorite concentration is 20 ∼ 400 μM [55]. At abnormally high concentrations of hypochlorite, the oxidative stress significantly increases, resulting in damage of protein, DNA and RNA [56]. Aberrant level of hypochlorite could cause diversified diseases, such as cancer, neuron degeneration, asthma and fibrillation [57–59]. So far numerous methods for determination of hypochlorite include ICP-AES, ICP-MS, electrochemical method, spectrophotometry [60], chemiluminescence [61], colorimetric method [62] and etc. [63,64]. However, there existed some disadvantages which could hardly be overcome in traditional methods mentioned above, such as complicated pretreatment, cockamamie operation, low sensitivity and high cost [65]. Fluorescence sensing platforms, including nanoclusters and metal organic frameworks [66–68], stepped forward and became outstanding alternative strategy for detecting hypochlorite over other methods. CDs with fluorescent emission have been widely utilized for monitoring hypochlorite due to their high sensitivity, good biocompatibility and low toxicity. Yin et al. [69] first developed CDs-based detection method of hypochlorite. Blue emissive CDs were synthesized whose fluorescence could be quenched by hypochlorite because of occurrence of surface oxidation, introducing surface oxidation mechanism in CDs-based hypochlorite detection and indicating a novel frontier of hypochlorite detection. Huang et al. [70] synthesized green-emissive CDs with excellent photostability for hypochlorite sensing based on surface oxidation, which were more safety to biology samples compared to blue-emissive CDs. Subsequently, different detection methods of hypochlorite derived from CDs were developed to monitor hypochlorite concentrations in environmental samples, but monitoring of hypochlorite in biology systems was still a poser. Li et al. [71] fabricated first blue-emissive CDs for cell imaging of hypochlorite, whose fluorescence significantly decayed in the presence of hypochlorite as result of redox reaction, whereas the short-wavelength emission property severely restricted their further application in biological imaging. Then green emissive fluorine-nitrogen co-doped CDs (F,N-CDs) for determination and cell imaging of hypochlorite were synthesized. The F,N-CDs possessed extremely low detection limit (8.2 nM) towards hypochlorite with outstanding selectivity, but could cause evident stimulation to biological samples and be easily interfered by auto-fluorescence effect of living cells [72]. Most of the fluorescent probes used for monitoring hypochlorite concentrations were short-wavelength probes with blue or green emission. They were lack of penetration capacity and could not avert auto-fluorescence effect. On the other hand, LW-CDs could offset these problems to a certain extent. Besides, most short-wavelength CDs designed to detect hypochlorite are more harmful to biological samples compared to LW-CDs. Up to now, more and more LW-CDs derived from different precursors through a variety of synthesis methods have been reported, and detection analytes based on LW-CDs have been more diversified [73–77]. Jiao et al. [78] proposed a facile strategy for preparation of nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped LW-CDs, which were ideal for detection and cellular imaging of hypochlorite, but the linear range was a little narrow (0.74–25.93 μM). It is still necessary to fabricate detection methods by LW-CDs in different ways, for example, colormetric and fluorescence dual-mode detection, ratiometric fluorescence detection or multi-substances analysis. Thus, in this study, we fabricated a multi-substances analysis method based on LW-CDs for detecting pH and hypochlorite.

Intracellular pH plays an important role in many metabolism and physiological processes, including signal transduction, cellular proliferation and apoptosis. Disorder of intracellular pH could lead to various pathological conditions, such as cancer, chronic kidney disease and arthritis [79–81]. Normal pH in healthy cells is 7.2 ∼ 7.4. Imbalance and fluctuation of pH is usually found in tissues with many secondary diseases [82]. Hitherto, many methods and technologies have been used into detection of intracellular pH, such as microelectrodes, nuclear magnetic resonance and absorption spectroscopy [83–85]. However, there are many shortcomings which could hardly be overcome in tradition methods, such as high cost, complicated sampling process and complex operations. Fluorescence detection has advantages of high sensitivity, low cost and simple detection process, and CDs-based fluorescence detection strategy has attracted massive attention because of low toxicity, low cost, high biocompatibility and fast detection [86]. Therefore, it is of great importance to develop detection method based on CDs to monitor change of intracellular pH value. Qu et al. [87] developed first fluorescence nanosensors based on blue emissive CDs with the ability to monitor fluctuations of temperature, pH and Fe(III) concentrations, which exploited a new domain of fluorescence sensing of pH. Subsequently, a sensing strategy derived from CDs with blue emission were established for detecting pH in both fluorescence and colorimetric ways, further enriching applications realm of CDs [88]. Hu et al. [89] synthesized blue-emissive sulfur-doped CDs for intracellular pH imaging with good linear relationship within pH 3–9, which made a contribution to CDs-based biosensing study of pH. Sun et al. [90] successfully fabricated red emissive CDs through solvothermal procedure. However, the purify process of the obtained CDs was complicated which needed to be through silica column chromatography. Our group [91] prepared orange emission carbon dots through nitrogen-doping and one-step hydrothermal strategy, and expanded the application of carbon dots for hypochlorite and pH detection, providing a novel idea for the use of long-wavelength carbon dots. Therefore, it is imperative to develop facile sensing method derived from LW-CDs for biosensing of pH.

Herein, we successfully synthesized LW-CDs for monitoring of hypochlorite and pH in living cells. The LW-CDs were synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal method based on L-cysteine (L-cys) and neutral red (NR). The fluorescence intensity of LW-CDs gradually decreased with the increase of pH value, and maintained double good linear relationships between pH values 4.2–5.8 and 5.8–7.4. What’s more, addition of hypochlorite into LW-CDs could make the fluorescence of LW-CDs decay owing to static quenching. The linear range was 10 ∼ 162.5 μM and the detection limit was 1.021 μM. LW-CDs could also be utilized as imaging reagents in Hela living cells owing excellent biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity. Therefore, the LW-CDs have promising application potential in hypochlorite sensing, pH fluctuation monitoring and cellular imaging.

In brief, 5.9 mg of NR and 11.8 mg of L-cys were added into 20 mL DI water. The solution was sonicated for 3 min, loaded into 50 mL of a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and heated at 180℃ for 4 h. After the reaction was completed, the solution was dialyzed with a 500–1000 Da dialysis membrane for 3 d, and LW-CDs powder was obtained after lyophilization.

BR buffer system was adopted to measure detection performance of LW-CDs to pH variation. First of all, we got a series of BR buffer solution, whose pH varied from 2 to 10 and pH interval was set as 0.2. And then 60 μL LW-CDs solution (0.26 mg/mL) was added into 2 mL BR buffer solution. The fluorescence spectra of the solution were measured to observe the relationship between pH and the maximum fluorescence intensity of LW-CDs solution.

60 μL LW-CDs solution (0.26 mg/mL) was added into 2 mL DI water. Then different contents of hypochlorite stock solution was gradually added into the LW-CDs solution (0–300 μM), respectively. The PL emission spectra were recorded at λex/λem of 520/587 nm.

The TEM image, size distribution and AFM image were displayed in Fig. 2 Clear features of regular spherical morphology were shown in Fig. 1
a. The average size of LW-CDs was 2.43 nm (Fig. 1b), and the nanoparticles of LW-CDs dispersed uniformly. The inset of Fig. 1a showed the crystal structure of LW-CDs, and the interlayer lattice spacing was 0.21 nm. AFM image (Fig. 1b) showed that the particle height of the LW-CDs was about 2.43 nm, which was consistent with the result of TEM. In conclusion, these results elucidated that the LW-CDs had features of uniform size distribution and good dispersity.

The FTIR and XPS spectrum was measured to probe surface structure of carbon dots. As shown in Fig. 2a, seven peaks could be observed at 614, 1101, 1405, 1630, 1711, and 3210 cm−1 respectively, which confirms the existence of C-S, −SO2-, C-O, C = N, C = C, –OH/–NH bond on the surface of the LW-CDs. The result XPS was shown in Fig. 2b. Four obvious binding energy peaks could be observed at 195, 282, 396 and 528 eV, which corresponds to S2p, C1s, N1s and O1s respectively. High-resolution spectrums of C1s, N1s, O1s and S2p were measured, which was displayed in Fig. 2c, 2d, 2e and 2f. C1s spectrum could be divided into three carbon species: C–C/C = C at 284.8 eV, C = O at 285.3 eV, and C-O at 287.25 eV. In N1s spectrum, three nitrogen species which represent pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and graphitic N were presented at 398.25 eV, 398.85 eV and 400.65 eV. And in the O1s spectrum, two oxygen species could be observed at 398.85 eV and 400.65 eV which confirms the existence of C-O and C = O on the surface of LW-CDs. Then in the S2p spectrum, we could see two peaks (S2p 1/2 at 167.65 eV and S2p 3/2 at 168.85 eV) which proved the LW-CDs are doped with sulfur.

The UV/Vis spectra and PL fluorescence spectra were displayed in Fig. 3 . Two peaks could be observed in the UV/Vis spectrum at 272 nm and 531 nm, which attributed to π → π* transition of the C = C bond and the n → π* transition of the C = O/C-N bond, respectively. The maximum excitation wavelength was 520 nm, while the maximum emission wavelength was 587 nm (Fig. 3a). As the excitation wavelength increased from 440 nm to 580 nm, there was no significant change in the emission wavelength of LW-CDs (Fig. 3b). The relative quantum yield was calculated as 2.53 % using rhodamine B as reference substance(Fig S2, S3). Although the quantum yield of the prepared CDs is relatively low, we believe that the fluorescence intensity-based detection method constructed is reliable due to the significant changes in the fluorescence intensity of CDs before and after the addition of the target substance [92–94]. As shown in Fig S4, the fluorescence intensity of LW-CDs remained relatively stable in different concentrations of KCl stock solutions. The results show that the fluorescence intensity can be kept stable when the KCl concentration is changed from 0 to 4 mol/L, which indicates that LW-CDs have excellent fluorescence stability to the solution ion concentration.

As a variety of functional groups existed on the surface of the LW-CDs, we measured the pH response of LW-CDs. As shown in Fig. 4 a, when pH varied from 2.0 to 10.0, the fluorescence of the LW-CDs showed an evident reduction. As shown in Fig. 4b, the Boltzmann equation was adopted to fit the change: y = 1100.65 + 2420.51/((1 + exp(x-6.302))/0.5239) (R2 = 0.9997), In addtion, it could be seen in Fig. 4c and 4d that there were two pH range that could be fitted: y = 5009.68–353.506x (R2 = 0.9987, linear range 4.2–5.8) and y = 9075.13–1071.036x (R2 = 0.9975, linear range 5.8–7.4). This result suggested that LW-CDs have the ability to monitor pH changes under physiological conditions. We deduced that the responsive mechanism was protonation and deprotonation of nitrogen-containing functional groups on surface of LW-CDs. As shown in Fig S1, the zeta potential of LW-CDs varied from –23.4 mV to 23.6 mV while pH changed from 4 to 12, which implied that fluorescence of LW-CDs responded to pH through protonation and deprotonation.

As shown in Fig. 5 a, with the increase of ClO- concentration (0–300 μM), fluorescence intensity of LW-CDs solution significantly decreased. As shown in Fig. 5b, Boltzmann equation was adopted to fit the curve (y = 0.8772–0.9058/(1 + exp((x-85.00)/31.62)), R2 = 0.9999). Fig. 5c showed the linear fitting equation (y = -0.0505 + 0.0058x, R2 = 0.9827). The linear range was 10 ∼ 162.5 μM and detection limit was 1.021 μM. 60 μL LW-CDs solution was added into 2 mL DI water and different ions were added (Fig. 5d). The results showed that hypochlorite could lead to significant fluorescence decay of the LW-CDs, while other ions could not cause evident fluorescence change of the LW-CDs, which meant the LW-CDs had good selection to hypochlorite. Compared with other carbon dot based ClO-fluorescence sensors, the as-mentioned LW-CDs have a longer emission wavelength and a wider linear range.

We measured the fluorescence lifetime of the LW-CDs to figure out the mechanism of fluorescence response to ClO-. In Fig. 5e, the fluorescence decay curves of LW-CDs solution and LW-CDs solution with 0.5 mM ClO- were displayed. After we added ClO- in LW-CDs solution, obvious change of fluorescence lifetime was not observed. Fluorescence lifetime of LW-CDs solution was 2.914 ns, while fluorescence lifetime of LW-CDs solution with ClO- was 2.865 ns. As shown in Fig S5, the KSV was calculated as 1.613 × 1012 L/mol/s, which is much larger than 2.0 × 1010 L/mol/s. Thus we deduced that the quenching mechanism was static quenching. Then we measured the absorbance spectrum of LW-CDs with and without hypochlorite. As shown in Fig. 5f, after hypochlorite was added into LW-CDs solution, two novel peaks appeared at 274 nm and 432 nm, which implied that the reaction between LW-CDs and hypochlorite generated new fluorescence structure. Therefore we deduced the mechanism of fluorescence response was static quenching.

We measured the toxicity of the LW-CDs to Hela cells. As shown in Fig S6, with the increase of concentration of the LW-CDs, the viability of cells gradually decreased. Cells viability of all concentration groups could maintain at a relative high level (higher than 80 %). Judged from the result, we deduced that the LW-CDs had relative low toxicity to cells and had promising application potential into biological monitoring and cells imaging.

In conclusion, long-wavelength emission carbon dots were successfully synthesized by a one-pot hydrothermal method of NR and L-cysteine. When the surrounding pH value varied from 4.2 to 7.4, the fluorescence of LW-CDs responded to the pH change and showed a good linear relationship. After adding ClO- into LW-CDs solution, the fluorescence intensity of LW-CDs significantly decayed via static quenching. The linear range was 10–162.5 μM and LOD was 1.021 μM. The as-proposed LW-CDs have been successfully used for intracellular pH and ClO- monitoring due to their low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility, implying the potential for biological applications of LW-CDs. The work further enriched the long-wavelength CDs theory and their applications in fluorescence monitoring and biological sensing.

Yating Meng: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Linzhu Wu: Investigation, Formal analysis. Junxiu Zhao: Supervision. Shaomin Shuang: Supervision. Chuan Dong: Supervision, Project administration. Jisheng Nie: Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 82073526 and 22274090).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.124767.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

Spectrochim. Acta A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.
